translation: I swear by the Day of Resurrection
commentary:
لَآ أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ

The word لَا at the beginning of this surah is not actually part of the sentence structure. Rather, it functions as a powerful exclamation, a divine interruption to all the false theories and misconceptions about the afterlife that were circulating among the Arabs. It's as if Allah ﷻ is saying, "Silence! All these ideas being thrown around are wrong."

When the Prophet ﷺ first began teaching, the disbelievers had various philosophies about what happens after death. Some believed in reincarnation, others thought there was no resurrection at all, and some imagined everyone would simply go to heaven. These different religious traditions and ideas floated through their poetry, songs and conversations. Even today, countless theories about the afterlife persist, from ghosts haunting houses to various spiritual beliefs that contradict Islamic teaching.

The Quran's response cuts through all of this with an oath: أُقْسِمُ بِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - "I swear by the Day of Resurrection." This oath can be understood as coming from Allah ﷻ Himself, or as a command to the Prophet ﷺ to go among the people and declare this truth. When the Messenger swears by the Day of Judgment, he makes himself part of the conversation. His integrity, his character, the Prophet's ﷺ very being stands as testimony that what he's about to say carries more weight and purpose than anything anyone else has proposed. The first evidence for the reality of Judgment Day is the integrity of the Prophet ﷺ himself.

translation: And I swear by the reproaching soul [to the certainty of resurrection]
commentary:
وَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلنَّفْسِ ٱللَّوَّامَةِ

The second oath introduces something profound: النَّفْسِ ٱللَّوَّامَةِ - the self that continuously blames itself. The word لَوَّامَة comes from لوم, meaning blame. The grammatical form indicates intensity and repetition: a self that keeps blaming and keeps blaming and keeps blaming.

This guilty conscience is brought into the discussion about resurrection, and the connection is remarkable. That guilt inside us is actually proof of Judgment Day. When you do something wrong and feel guilty inside, that feeling serves as an internal moral alarm system. You've tripped the alarm and it goes off. This guilt is essentially Allah's ﷻ way of reminding you through your own conscience that consequences exist, that accountability is real. The ultimate consequence for all deeds happens on Judgment Day, and the alarm for every deed that will count on that day already goes off inside you now. It's called guilt.

You might not get caught, you might not face worldly punishment, but you still feel bad. Why? Because you're subconsciously programmed to recognize that accountability is coming. Guilt is such a powerful human emotion, and critically, it's not taught. You cannot teach a child to feel guilt. It's not a cultural construct where some societies have it and others don't. Guilt is a universal human emotion that exists across all of humanity.

However, guilt can also be killed. It can be removed from your psyche. When someone commits enough crimes, when they desensitize themselves through repeated wrongdoing, النَّفْس اللَّوَّامَة (the self that blames) becomes النَّفْس الأَمَّارَة (the self that commands evil). The conscience stops functioning. There's no more feeling bad, no more internal discomfort, no more remorse. The person simply does what they want without thinking twice.

Every emotion Allah ﷻ placed inside us has its purpose, even the so-called negative ones. Fear prevents us from walking into danger and helps us fear the consequences of the Hereafter. Sadness allows us to feel the pain of others, making us merciful and compassionate. Even guilt, which can be overwhelming, has its rightful place in making us healthy, functioning human beings.

The key is what we do with guilt. Consider two students who both fail an exam with the same score. One sits crying, paralyzed by guilt, asking himself why he didn't study, why he wasted his time. The other also feels that same pain, but wipes his tears and says, "I need more practice. I'm not going to stop until I get this right." Both feel guilty, but one allows the negative emotion to paralyze him while the other uses it to drive himself toward improvement.

When Prophet Musa (AS) accidentally killed a man, that grief could have destroyed him. Depression alone can be lethal. But Musa (AS) understood that he had to learn from his mistakes and move forward. He turned to Allah ﷻ and said, رَبِّ إِنِّى لِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ إِلَىَّ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَقِيرٌ - "My Lord, I am in desperate need of whatever good You send my way." He became more eager than ever to perform good deeds, understanding that to compensate for that terrible mistake, he would seize every opportunity to do right.

This is the النَّفْس اللَّوَّامَة in its proper function. The conscience that keeps poking, that keeps you feeling uncomfortable about your wrongs, isn't meant to be an enjoyable experience. Most people respond by burying their guilt, rationalizing their behavior: "Yes, I said something wrong, but they said it first," or "It happened because of this situation." There's always a chain of reasons to cover guilt. But Allah's ﷻ religion asks us to be true to ourselves, to acknowledge our real guilt and do something constructive about it instead of covering it up.

When both of these realities exist within the human conscience - first, the character and example of the Prophet ﷺ, and second, this natural guilt mechanism - then understanding that a day is coming when all our deeds will be brought before us makes complete sense. All that guilt that kept poking us, saying "You better do a good deed to compensate for this bad deed so you don't get in serious trouble one day," will all come to fruition. But if someone has killed their conscience, if they have no guilt left inside them, then talk of Judgment Day seems meaningless to them.

translation: Does man think that We will not assemble his bones
commentary:
أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَلَّن نَّجْمَعَ عِظَامَهُۥ

Why do I love these ayahs so much? Because they teach us that our religion isn't just a cold computation of logical proofs where you believe in Allah ﷻ based purely on rational arguments. Human beings were created far more complex than pure ABC logic and rationale. Yes, there's tremendous rationality in our religion, abundant common sense, profound intellectual depth. But Allah ﷻ knows His creation. He created human beings with emotions, with a psychology of feelings that cannot be computed or reduced to equations. You cannot calculate guilt mathematically.

There are aspects of a human being coming to guidance that are internal, emotional, deeply personal. These become proofs that work inside you, demonstrating that this is truth. These aren't the kind of proofs you use in a philosophical debate with someone else, but they're real proofs nonetheless, proofs that Allah ﷻ placed within your very being.

Now Allah ﷻ addresses the intellectual objection: أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ - "Does the human being assume, does he calculate, does he think that We will not gather his bones together?" The critics of resurrection used to argue that after death, when bodies decay into rotten dirt and crumbled bones that collapse when you try to pick them up, how could anyone be raised again from that state?

translation: Yes. [We are] Able [even] to proportion his fingertips
commentary:
بَلَىٰ قَـٰدِرِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّسَوِّىَ بَنَانَهُۥ

The response is emphatic: بَلَىٰ - "No, on the contrary!" Then comes the stunning declaration: قَـٰدِرِينَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّسَوِّىَ بَنَانَهُۥ - "We are completely capable that We can fashion and even out every single one of his fingertips."

Forget about just putting bones back together. Allah ﷻ will reconstruct you down to your last fingertip, down to the most intricate detail. The word بَنَان means fingertips, and interestingly, this is the origin of the English word "banana," which came from the Muslim world. When English speakers saw the fruit's shape resembling a long finger, they adopted the Arabic term.

The specificity of mentioning fingertips is profound. Allah ﷻ isn't just saying He can reassemble the major bones and body parts. He's declaring His ability to recreate the finest details, the unique patterns, the smallest features that make you distinctly you.

translation: But man desires to continue in sin
commentary:
بَلْ يُرِيدُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُۥ

The word بَل shifts the discussion: "However, rather, the reality is..." What's the real issue here? يُرِيدُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُۥ - "The human being intends to continue doing whatever he wants right in front of him."

The word فُجُور comes from فَجَرَ, which literally means explosion. فَجْر, the dawn, is the explosion of light breaking through darkness. A فاجر is someone who explodes, someone who has no control over their whims and desires. Whatever impulse comes into their thoughts immediately comes out in their actions. Whatever energy they have, whatever urge strikes them, they follow it without restraint. This is uncontrolled outburst, impulsive behavior.

لِيَفْجُرَ أَمَامَهُۥ - "Whatever is right in front of him, whatever impulse he has, he wants to follow it immediately." Human beings are fundamentally impulsive, and that's precisely why they don't want to think about Judgment Day. Guilt says "Don't do this," but النَّفْس الأَمَّارَة says "Come on, do it now! Why are you waiting? Live today, you don't know about tomorrow!"

translation: He asks, "When is the Day of Resurrection
commentary:
يَسْـَٔلُ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ

The impulsive person dismissively asks: أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - "When is the Day of Resurrection?" If this were a genuine question asked with sincerity, the Quran would use مَتَى, which indicates genuine curiosity and a desire to know. But أَيَّانَ carries mockery within it. It's sarcastic: "Oh come on, Judgment Day? Really? You're worried about that?"

Picture the young person with their whole life ahead of them. They have freedom, money, opportunities. The overbearing control of parents is gone. They're living independently in a city where nobody knows what they do at night. An impulse comes and they just do whatever they want. And when someone mentions Judgment Day? "Yeah, yeah, I know about Judgment Day... but right now... I'll deal with that later, tomorrow, insha'Allah." أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - "When is it coming? I'll worry about it when it gets here."

translation: So when vision is dazzled
commentary:
فَإِذَا بَرِقَ ٱلْبَصَرُ

Now Allah ﷻ describes what that Day will actually be like. فَإِذَا بَرِقَ ٱلْبَصَرُ - "When the eyes are flashed, when they're shocked into a wide stare." The word بَرِقَ comes from بَرْق, meaning lightning. It describes eyes that bulge out of fear, that freeze in shock. This is the look of absolute terror, when something so overwhelming strikes that the eyes become fixed, unable to look away.

translation: And the moon darkens
commentary:
وَخَسَفَ ٱلْقَمَرُ

وَخَسَفَ ٱلْقَمَرُ - "And the moon loses its light, it goes dark." Notice the parallel with the previous surah, which mentioned the brightness of the moon illuminating the night. Now that same moon will be extinguished.

translation: And the sun and the moon are joined
commentary:
وَجُمِعَ ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ

وَجُمِعَ ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ - "And the sun and the moon are brought together, smashed into one another." The celestial bodies that govern our sense of time and order will collide, marking the end of the world as we know it.

translation: Man will say on that Day, "Where is the [place of] escape
commentary:
يَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ أَيْنَ ٱلْمَفَرُّ

يَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ - "The human being will say on that day..." What will they say? أَيْنَ ٱلْمَفَرُّ - "Where do I run? Where is the escape? Where is the place to flee to?" The desperate outcry of someone seeking any possible refuge.

translation: No! There is no refuge
commentary:
كَلَّا لَا وَزَرَ

The response is absolute: كَلَّا - "No! Absolutely not!" لَا وَزَرَ - "There is no refuge, no place to hide." The word وَزَر comes from a root meaning barrier or protection. It refers to something like a bunker that shields you from attack. Allah ﷻ declares there will be no bunker, no barrier, no place that can cover for you or protect you from what's coming.

translation: To your Lord, that Day, is the [place of] permanence
commentary:
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمُسْتَقَرُّ

إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمُسْتَقَرُّ - "Toward your Master on that day is the place where you must stand." المُسْتَقَرّ means the place where you have to remain, where you must stay. There's nowhere else to go. Every soul will stand before their Lord.

translation: Man will be informed that Day of what he sent ahead and kept back
commentary:
يُنَبَّؤُا۟ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بِمَا قَدَّمَ وَأَخَّرَ

This ayah contains one of the most life-transforming concepts in the Quran. يُنَبَّؤُا۟ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ - "The human being will be informed thoroughly, interrogated in detail." The verb form indicates comprehensive notification, complete information being given.

بِمَا قَدَّمَ وَأَخَّرَ - This phrase is often translated as "what he sent forward and what he left behind," but there's a more powerful interpretation: What did he make a priority? What did he put on the backburner?

The previous surah told us that whoever wants can take a step forward, and whoever wants can stay back (فَمَن شَآءَ ٱتَّخَذَ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ سَبِيلًا). Now we're told that on Judgment Day, the human being will be informed: What took priority for you? What was number one? What took precedence in your life? And what were the things you kept saying could wait?

The crime of the human being isn't necessarily that they never do good deeds. It's that they say good deeds can wait. The problem isn't always that people don't leave sin - it's that they say "I'll leave it later. Let me do what I want right now. I have time."

Think about your priorities. What actually came first for you in life? What came later? Did you prioritize memorizing the Quran, or did you prioritize watching season after season of television shows? You had dreams, intentions, goals - what did you actually do about them? How many times did you say "One day, when my heart is purified, then I'll start"?

There's another layer to قَدَّمَ and أَخَّرَ. Every deed you do is being sent forward. Your actions are being processed, filed, recorded, and you're going to meet those deeds on Judgment Day. You don't meet your deeds now - you just perform them. But you will stand face to face with them then.

If your prayer was performed carelessly, you'll be standing in front of that careless prayer, staring right at it. If you were lying, cheating, backbiting, angry, arrogant, condescending - whatever you were, you'll be looking at it right in your face. And you'll say مَا لِهَـٰذَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ - "What is this record?" (18:49)

translation: Rather, man, against himself, will be a witness
commentary:
بَلِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ بَصِيرَةٌ

But it's not as if the human being is blind to all this now. بَلِ - "Rather, the reality is..." ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ بَصِيرَةٌ - "The human being, against his own self, is fully insightful."

There is one person who knows so much about you that nobody else knows, besides Allah ﷻ - and that's you. You have insight into who you are. You know your flaws, your limitations, your capabilities. You know your strengths and your weaknesses. You know which opportunities you take advantage of and which ones you get lazy about. You understand yourself better than anyone else possibly could.

Yet you and I choose to lie to ourselves. We decide we're not going to have an honest conversation with ourselves about ourselves, or about ourselves with Allah ﷻ. We don't want that honest dialogue.

When was the last time you were driving alone and just talked to Allah ﷻ? You can talk in English - it's okay, and one day in Arabic, insha'Allah. But have that conversation: "I've done some pretty messed up things." Spell them out. List them until you cry. "I want to change. I don't want to do that anymore. Help me change. I don't want to make excuses. I don't want to be that person."

You are in full view of yourself. You know exactly what you're capable of. What we do as human beings is put up a front, construct a facade for other people, create an image of ourselves for others. Of course, I don't lay my sins out to you and you don't display your sins to me - that's how we're supposed to operate socially. But with yourself, with Allah ﷻ, there can be no facade. بَصِيرَةٌ - you have complete insight into your own reality.

This is the foundation of accountability: we cannot claim ignorance about ourselves. We cannot say we didn't know. Deep down, we always knew.

translation: Even if he presents his excuses
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَلْقَىٰ مَعَاذِيرَهُۥ

This honesty is difficult because we all have a self-defense mechanism. When criticized, we immediately defend ourselves. Someone points out a flaw, and we respond, "Why would you say that?" Even when we know something is wrong, we follow up with "but" and then launch into a paragraph of justification. "Why did you do that?" "I know I shouldn't have, but you should consider..." And another paragraph follows.

There's always a rationale, always an excuse. But here's the reality: give your rationale to the judge, give it to the police officer, give it to your teacher, to your parents, to your spouse. Don't give it to yourself. To yourself, stop throwing excuses.

The first thing Prophet Adam (AS) was taught when he came to earth was admission. This is profoundly powerful. Allah ﷻ said, "I will send you guidance," but before that instruction, the first thing revealed to him was: رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا—"Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves." Admit it. Before Allah ﷻ taught him to ask for forgiveness, He taught him to admit. The request for forgiveness came after: وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا—"And if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us." You must admit first; otherwise, your request for forgiveness is empty and hollow.

This is why the Day of Arafah is not just يوم عرفة (the Day of Arafah)—it's the day of اعتراف (confession), the day of admission. Admit to yourself. Accept and acknowledge who you are before Allah ﷻ, and then ask for forgiveness.

وَلَوْ أَلْقَىٰ مَعَاذِيرَهُۥ—Even if he keeps throwing all kinds of excuses, they won't help him.

translation: Move not your tongue with it, [O Muhammad], to hasten with recitation of the Qur'an
commentary:
لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِۦ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِۦٓ

Now the subject shifts dramatically. Allah ﷻ turns His attention to the Prophet ﷺ: لَا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِۦ لِسَانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِۦٓ—Don't rush your tongue to acquire it quickly. Acquire what quickly? The Quran.

The Prophet ﷺ would rehearse the Quran constantly. We must understand the context: this is over a span of 13 years in Makkah, and two-thirds of the Quran was revealed during this period. Yet the Prophet ﷺ didn't read or write. Imagine the magnitude—a tremendous amount of Quran coming down, and he had to know which surah goes where, which ayah belongs in which surah, because complete surahs didn't descend all at once. Surah Al-A'raf was coming, Surah Al-An'am was coming—both long surahs—while Surah Al-Muzzammil was being completed, and Surah Al-Ankabut was starting to be revealed. He had to keep track of all this in his chest, knowing exactly where each ayah belonged. So he rehearsed constantly.

Why does Allah ﷻ change the subject here? He was discussing the guilty human being, and suddenly He addresses the Prophet ﷺ about not rushing. Some scholars note this connection: the human being rushes toward sin, but the Prophet ﷺ is rushing toward good deeds—toward memorizing the Quran. Yet even rushing toward good deeds can be harmful. Don't rush through life. Take your time with things. Be patient with yourself.

translation: Indeed, upon Us is its collection [in your heart] and [to make possible] its recitation
commentary:
إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُۥ وَقُرْءَانَهُۥ

إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا جَمْعَهُۥ وَقُرْءَانَهُۥ—No doubt, We have taken upon Ourselves its gathering and its recitation. This ayah contains profound implications about the Quran's preservation. The 114 surahs in the precise order we have them—this is one of the proofs within the Quran itself that demonstrates its divinely ordained sequence.

The structure here is critical. إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا establishes exclusivity—We have taken exclusively upon Ourselves. The phrase عَلَيْنَا (upon Us) doesn't just indicate responsibility; it indicates that this responsibility belongs to no one else. The gathering of the Quran—what we historically attribute to the companions (RA) who reached consensus on its compilation, and then the mushaf that was assembled during the time of Uthman (RA)—all of this was preceded by a divine plan that Allah ﷻ revealed in this ayah.

جَمْعَهُۥ—its gathering. This isn't merely collection; it's the precise arrangement and sequencing. وَقُرْءَانَهُۥ—and its recitation. When you recite something from beginning to end without alteration, the order is fixed. If Allah ﷻ says He has taken responsibility for having the entire Quran recited, this was fulfilled at the end of the Prophet's ﷺ life when he recited the complete mushaf with Angel Jibreel (AS) twice. In what order did he recite? The very order we have today, because Allah ﷻ took responsibility for gathering it precisely this way.

translation: So when We have recited it [through Gabriel], then follow its recitation
commentary:
فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَـٰهُ فَٱتَّبِعْ قُرْءَانَهُۥ

فَإِذَا قَرَأْنَـٰهُ فَٱتَّبِعْ قُرْءَانَهُۥ—Then when We read it onto you—meaning through the Angel—just follow its recital. Follow along. We'll make sure it stays in your heart. We'll take care of the memory concern. You simply take care of following along.

translation: Then upon Us is its clarification [to you]
commentary:
ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُۥ

ثُمَّ إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا بَيَانَهُۥ—Then, above and beyond that, it is Our responsibility exclusively to make sure it's clearly understood. Its explanation, its interpretation—We've taken responsibility that it will be properly understood.

Some scholars explain بَيَانَهُۥ in another dimension: sometimes a book is preserved, but the way it's recited isn't. Sometimes the words are preserved, but their pronunciation and how pronunciation affects meaning isn't maintained. If you've learned anything about Arabic, you know it's extraordinarily sensitive to these details. The difference between حَرِيق (a fire) and حَرِيقَة can change everything. Allah ﷻ took responsibility for maintaining this clarity.

What other religion can claim that after centuries upon centuries, the book remains as clear as it was originally? That it's understood the same way it was understood by earlier generations? That it's recited letter-by-letter identically? The system of Tajweed itself is miraculous—the preservation of how each letter should be pronounced. You don't say مَدْرَسَة, you say مَدْرَسَه. Down to the specific application of rules: don't do that here, do it there. What other text has an entire science dedicated to its pronunciation? There's no physics textbook with a science of pronunciation. No other literature has this.

translation: No! But you love the immediate
commentary:
كَلَّا بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ

كَلَّا—No, no, no. Allah ﷻ returns to addressing humanity. The Prophet ﷺ was rushing to memorize the Quran, but بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱلْعَاجِلَةَ—you people love to rush toward immediate gratification. These petty temptations, these fleeting desires—this is what you rush toward.

translation: And leave the Hereafter
commentary:
وَتَذَرُونَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ

وَتَذَرُونَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ—And you cast away the Hereafter. You leave it behind. The Akhirah becomes an afterthought for you.

translation: [Some] faces, that Day, will be radiant
commentary:
وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ

وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ—Some faces on that Day will be radiant. The grammatical structure here uses an indefinite construction (وُجُوهٌ) to magnify these faces. Look at these faces! There are some faces, some truly special faces, that on that Day will be lit up, illuminated, glowing.

translation: Looking at their Lord
commentary:
إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ

إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ—Looking toward their Master. The word نَاظِرَةٌ comes from نَظَر, which means to stare intently at something, unable to take your eyes away. They will be gazing at Allah ﷻ, their vision fixed upon Him.

translation: And [some] faces, that Day, will be contorted
commentary:
وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَاسِرَةٌ

وَوُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بَاسِرَةٌ—And there are faces on that Day that will be covered in extreme sadness, consumed by gloom.

translation: Expecting that there will be done to them [something] backbreaking
commentary:
تَظُنُّ أَن يُفْعَلَ بِهَا فَاقِرَةٌ

تَظُنُّ أَن يُفْعَلَ بِهَا فَاقِرَةٌ—They will be convinced that any moment now, a catastrophic calamity is about to befall them. Imagine someone in complete darkness who hears a terrifying sound but cannot identify its source or direction. They know something dreadful is approaching, but they don't know what or from where. That's the terror etched on their faces.

translation: No! When the soul has reached the collar bones
commentary:
كَلَّآ إِذَا بَلَغَتِ ٱلتَّرَاقِىَ

Now we transition from Judgment Day to the journey that leads there. There's a saying, attributed by some to the Prophet ﷺ and by others to the Companions (RA): مَن مَاتَ فَقَدْ قَامَتْ قِيَامَتُهُ—Whoever dies, their Qiyamah has begun. When does your Qiyamah begin? When does my Qiyamah begin? It begins at death.

Yawm Al-Qiyamah comes from قيام (standing). When does this "standing" begin? It begins when we can no longer stand, when we lie down permanently. Our standing begins when we cease to stand—the profound irony of it.

كَلَّآ—No, no, no. إِذَا بَلَغَتِ ٱلتَّرَاقِىَ—When it—meaning the soul—reaches the collarbone. It reaches the top of the chest, right here. You can feel it leaving; the train is departing the station.

translation: And it is said, "Who will cure [him]
commentary:
وَقِيلَ مَنْ ۜ رَاقٍ

وَقِيلَ مَنْ رَاقٍ—And it is shouted: "Is there any healer?" The passive voice here (قِيلَ—"it is said") captures the chaos—it's not just one person speaking. His cousin is shouting, his mother, his daughter, his son, his wife—everyone is screaming: "Doctor! Doctor! Doctor! Where is the nurse?" The word رَاقٍ comes from رُقْيَة—who will give him medicine? Who will heal him? Who will protect him?

translation: And the dying one is certain that it is the [time of] separation
commentary:
وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ ٱلْفِرَاقُ

وَظَنَّ أَنَّهُ ٱلْفِرَاقُ—And he realizes, it has settled upon him: it's time to depart. While everyone around him is crying and screaming for doctors, he's looking at their faces, understanding this is the last time he'll see them. It's time to go. He's also looking at his own body, coming to terms with the reality: this body is no longer mine. My soul is about to be extracted. The moment of separation has arrived.

translation: And the leg is wound about the leg
commentary:
وَٱلْتَفَّتِ ٱلسَّاقُ بِٱلسَّاقِ

وَٱلْتَفَّتِ ٱلسَّاقُ بِٱلسَّاقِ—And the leg wraps around the leg. This phrase carries such weight. In classical Arabic poetry, this expression describes a camel walking through the desert that hasn't had water for an extended period. Camels have large water reserves, but even those eventually run out. The camel continues walking, each step more labored than the last, until its long legs—weakened and trembling—become intertwined, and it collapses. Those final steps before its legs lock together and it tips over are described as التفت الساق بالساق.

This describes the dying person in two scenarios: He's standing when suddenly his legs lock into each other and he collapses—a heart attack, and he's gone. Or he's lying in bed, the soul having reached the collarbone, people crying all around him. In his pain, the patient rubs his legs together, one foot over the other, unable to lie still. This too is التفت الساق بالساق.

translation: To your Lord, that Day, will be the procession
commentary:
إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمَسَاقُ

إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ ٱلْمَسَاقُ—To your Master alone, on that Day, is the place of driving. Remember the linguistic pattern: المساق comes from ساق يسوق—to drive or steer. The soul doesn't simply leave and float aimlessly. It is being steered, driven to a specific destination. It's being directed to the place where it must go.

translation: And the disbeliever had not believed, nor had he prayed
commentary:
فَلَا صَدَّقَ وَلَا صَلَّىٰ

Now that he's gone, Allah ﷻ comments on what this person was like while still in his body. فَلَا صَدَّقَ—He didn't confirm the truth. وَلَا صَلَّىٰ—And he didn't pray.

Look at how these are paired. He didn't confirm the truth, and the evidence of this internal state? He didn't pray. The external manifestation of denying truth is abandoning prayer. Some scholars say this describes the specific case of Abu Jahl, but others note it applies more broadly.

translation: But [instead], he denied and turned away
commentary:
وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ

وَلَـٰكِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ—Rather, instead of confirming the truth, he declared it all a lie. And he turned away. What did he turn away from? From Salah.

When we think of Muslims today—yes, they're Muslims, they just don't pray—and we have people in our own families who don't pray, we must internalize the gravity of this. Allah ﷻ equated accepting truth with establishing prayer, and walking away from Salah with denying truth, calling it all a lie and a farce.

If we truly grasped this seriousness for ourselves, we wouldn't become complacent about it. We would persistently find ways—one approach after another—to remind our loved ones. If one method doesn't work, we'd find another way. Maybe they find us annoying, maybe they think we're condescending or self-righteous. We'd find a different approach, and then another, and another. But they need to receive this message, because Allah ﷻ made this connection explicit.

translation: And then he went to his people, swaggering [in pride]
commentary:
ثُمَّ ذَهَبَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ يَتَمَطَّىٰٓ

ثُمَّ ذَهَبَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ يَتَمَطَّىٰٓ—Then he went to his family walking with pride and arrogance. He returned home full of himself, with grand thoughts about his accomplishments, as if he had achieved something magnificent. He went back to his family to share what he considered his great successes of the day.

But what was his true tragedy? He didn't pray. For a believer, missing Salah should feel like losing a family member—that level of grief. Yet we wake up three hours after Fajr and think, "Oh well, there goes another one. I guess I'll just make it up later." And when we do make it up, what does that look like? We know exactly what that looks like.

## Ayah 34-35

أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰ / ثُمَّ أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰٓ

أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰ—Woe to you, and woe! ثُمَّ أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ فَأَوْلَىٰٓ—Then woe to you, and woe!

How close destruction has come to you, and it has come close again! This repetition—four times—carries multiple interpretations. Some scholars say this represents four stages of curse: Allah ﷻ curses this person while he's alive, then curses him when he's dying, then curses him when he's being resurrected, then curses him as he enters the Fire. أَوْلَىٰ لَكَ—again and again and again.

translation: Woe to you, and woe
Then woe to you, and woe
Does man think that he will be left neglected
commentary:
أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَن يُتْرَكَ سُدًى

أَيَحْسَبُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَن يُتْرَكَ سُدًى—Does the human being think he will be left without purpose? That he'll be left alone, aimless, free to do whatever he wants without accountability?

The word سُدًى means purposeless, ignored, left to one's own devices without consequence or responsibility. Human beings delude themselves into thinking they'll be left like this.

translation: Had he not been a sperm from semen emitted
commentary:
أَلَمْ يَكُ نُطْفَةً مِّن مَّنِىٍّ يُمْنَىٰ

أَلَمْ يَكُ نُطْفَةً مِّن مَّنِىٍّ يُمْنَىٰ—Was he not a drop of fluid emitted? If Allah ﷻ wanted to leave someone alone without purpose, He could have left him as mere fluid.

Think about it: Wasn't he himself a drop—a filthy fluid that no one wants to touch? That's what we were. A drop of fluid that gets deposited into a mother's womb.

translation: Then he was a clinging clot, and [Allah] created [his form] and proportioned [him]
commentary:
ثُمَّ كَانَ عَلَقَةً فَخَلَقَ فَسَوَّىٰ

ثُمَّ كَانَ عَلَقَةً—Then he became a clinging clot. فَخَلَقَ—Then Allah ﷻ created him. فَسَوَّىٰ—And fashioned him into perfect proportion.

Look at yourself. Next time you see a drop of fluid, imagine: I was that. Except the drop I originated from was filthy, something nobody wants to touch. And from that, I was created into a fully functional human being. So much work went into creating you—so much design, so much perfection, such meticulous detail. Every fingerprint, every feature, so articulate, so precisely formed.

And then you think you're just going to be left alone? That after all this intricate creation, there's no purpose, no accountability? Is that really what you believe?

translation: And made of him two mates, the male and the female
commentary:
فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ ٱلزَّوْجَيْنِ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰٓ

فَجَعَلَ مِنْهُ ٱلزَّوْجَيْنِ ٱلذَّكَرَ وَٱلْأُنثَىٰٓ—And out of that one drop, He created the two kinds: male and female. What is this saying? Oh, you think you're off the hook because you're a woman and these warnings are directed at men? No, you're not exempt. Both genders emerged from that same humble origin.

translation: Is not that [Creator] Able to give life to the dead
commentary:
أَلَيْسَ ذَٰلِكَ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ

أَلَيْسَ ذَٰلِكَ بِقَـٰدِرٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يُحْـِۧىَ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ—Is not that One capable of giving life to the dead?

The Prophet ﷺ would recite this ayah and respond: بَلَىٰ—Yes, of course He is. Of course You are, O Allah ﷻ! You are capable of all things.
